sound_type: mechanical click -- spine-and-groove snap on thorax, completely novel sound-production mechanism, percussive, brief, singular
ecosystem: Dead wood. Larvae in rotting logs and dead wood. Adults on vegetation, bark, and forest floor.
season: Spring through autumn. Adults active in warmer months.
order: Coleoptera (NEW ORDER -- beetles, largest order of insects, ~400,000 species)

coordinates.description: Reference for dead wood habitat in PNW forests, Washington. Click beetles found throughout forested lowlands and foothills.

taxonomy_note.description: NEW FAMILY (48th): Elateridae. NEW ORDER: Coleoptera (beetles -- the largest order of insects, approximately 400,000 described species, roughly 25% of all known animal species). Sixth insect order in the engine, joining Orthoptera, Hemiptera, Diptera, Plecoptera, Ephemeroptera.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Elateridae from Greek elater ('driver') -- referring to the springing/clicking mechanism. Coleoptera from Greek koleos ('sheath') + pteron ('wing') -- sheathed wings (elytra). Common name 'click beetle' for the audible click produced during the righting mechanism.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Elateridae is a large family with approximately 10,000 species worldwide. Multiple genera present in the PNW. Family-level entry.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Coleoptera -- NEW ORDER. Beetles. The largest order of insects. ~400,000 species. Sixth insect order in the engine.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Insecta -- continuing. Twelfth insect entry area (87-99 range with gaps). The insect era extends through degree 99.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Elateridae is the 48th family in the engine. NEW. First Coleoptera. Dead wood habitat connects to pileated woodpecker (54) and beaver (62).

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Broadband click. No tonal frequency. The snap produces a brief broadband impulse, not a sustained tone.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Broadband. Short-duration impulse with energy across a wide frequency range.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Not applicable. Single click events. Not a song or sustained call.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual click: <0.01 seconds. Instantaneous snap. May click repeatedly when handled.
song_metrics.note_structure: Single percussive click. No tonal structure. A mechanical snap.
song_metrics.note_types: Mechanical click (escape/righting mechanism). Not a mating call, not a territorial signal.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: No phrase structure. Individual clicks. May click repeatedly in rapid succession when disturbed.
song_metrics.timbre: Sharp, dry, percussive. A snap. No resonance, no sustain, no tonal quality. The sound of a mechanical latch releasing.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=2. Quiet. Audible at close range (within ~1 meter). Brief. Not carrying. The stonefly (94, E=2) is similarly quiet.
song_metrics.tempo: No tempo. Individual click events. If disturbed repeatedly, clicks may occur in rapid bursts.
song_metrics.context: ESCAPE MECHANISM. The click is produced when the beetle is on its back and needs to right itself. The spine-and-groove snap launches it into the air. Defensive, not communicative.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Multiple Elateridae species in the PNW. Click mechanism is family-wide. Sound character similar across species.
song_metrics.key_property: MECHANICAL CLICK -- the 99th acoustic element (species side). Completely novel sound-production mechanism. Not stridulation, not tymbal, not wing-buzz. Spine-and-groove mechanical snap. Fourth insect sound mechanism in the engine. E=2. Dead wood habitat. NEW FAMILY (48th). NEW ORDER: Coleoptera. Thirty-ninth consecutive original CSV.

physical_description.overall: Elongate, somewhat flattened beetle. 10-30 mm depending on species. Hard elytra (wing covers). Brown to black coloration, some species with patterns. Distinctive prosternal spine visible on underside.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 99: (1) ELONGATE BODY -- flattened, parallel-sided. (2) HARD ELYTRA -- wing covers. (3) PROSTERNAL SPINE -- visible on underside, the clicking mechanism. (4) CLICKING BEHAVIOR -- when placed on back, snaps into the air. (5) FOUND IN/NEAR DEAD WOOD.
physical_description.skin_detail: Hard exoskeleton (elytra). Brown to black. Some species with metallic or patterned elytra. Smooth or finely punctured surface.
physical_description.dental_detail: Mandibles for feeding. Adults feed on plant material, pollen, nectar. Larvae (wireworms) feed on roots, seeds, decaying wood.
physical_description.field_id: Elongate, flattened beetle found near dead wood or on vegetation. Brown to black. When placed on its back, snaps into the air with an audible click -- diagnostic behavior. The click is unmistakable.

nodes.title: Mechanical Click -- 99th Acoustic Element (species side)
nodes.description: Novel sound-production mechanism. Spine-and-groove snap on thorax. Percussive, brief, singular. E=2. Fourth insect sound mechanism in the engine. Not stridulation, not tymbal, not wing-buzz.

nodes.title: Coleoptera -- NEW ORDER (beetles arrive)
nodes.description: Largest order of insects. ~400,000 species. 25% of known animals. Sixth insect order in engine. Elateridae (click beetles) as the first representative.

nodes.title: Fourth Insect Sound Mechanism
nodes.description: Stridulation (87,88,98), tymbal (89), wing-buzz (91), mechanical click (99). Four distinct mechanisms. Each produces fundamentally different sound.

nodes.title: Dead Wood Habitat -- Ecological Connections
nodes.description: Larvae in rotting logs. Connects to pileated woodpecker (54, excavates dead wood) and beaver (62, creates dead wood). Dead wood as acoustic ecosystem.

nodes.title: The Kaina Contrast -- Novel Mechanisms
nodes.description: Both degree 99 entries involve novel mechanisms. Click beetle: mechanical sound. Kaina: cultural synthesis. Mechanical novelty and cultural novelty.

cross_references.relationship: INSECT E=2 SHARED. Both E=2 insects. Stonefly drumming (94) and click beetle snap (99). Both quiet, close-range. Different mechanisms -- abdomen drumming vs thorax snap.
E=2 SHARED. Mosquito wing-buzz (91, E=2) and click beetle snap (99, E=2). Both non-vocal, both E=2. Wing-buzz is sustained; click is percussive.
INSECT MECHANISM CONTRAST. Cricket stridulation (87) vs click beetle mechanical snap (99). Sustained tonal vs brief percussive. Two orders, two mechanisms.
DEAD WOOD HABITAT. Beaver chewing (62) creates dead wood. Click beetle larvae develop in dead wood. Ecological connection. The beaver builds the habitat.
DEAD WOOD HABITAT. Pileated woodpecker (54) excavates dead wood for insects. Click beetle larvae live in dead wood. Predator-prey acoustic ecology.
